title: 第 59 節
en: 3 " Brihaspati," says the Aitareya-brahmana, " was the Purohita of the gods, and the Purohitas of human kings are his successors.* i i 4 488 PUROHITAS.
" The king before whom there walks a priest, lives well established in his own house ; to him the earth yields for ever, and before him the people bow of their own accord.
" Unopposed he conquers treasures, those of his enemies and his friends, himself a king, who makes presents to a Brahman : the gods protect him."

This shows that the position of the Brahmans at the courts of the Kshatriya kings was more influential than that of mere chaplains. They walked before the king, and considered themselves superior to him. In later times, when the performance of the ceremonies no longer devolved on the Purohita, the chief priest took the place of the socalled Brahman priest, who was the episcopos of the whole, though he himself took little active part in it. Thus at the sacrifice of" Harischandra , described in the Aitareya-brahmana (vii. 16.), Ayasya acts as Udgatri, Jamadagni as Adhvaryu, Visvamitra as Hotri, and Vasishtha, who is known as the Purohita of the Ikshvaku dynasty, as Brahman. In the Taittiriya-sanhita (iii. 5. 2), we read: "Men were born, having a Vasishtha for Purohita, and therefore a Vasishtha is to be chosen as Brahman." In the Aitareya-brahmana again the Brahman is identified with Brihaspati, who was the Purohita, or pura-etri of the gods.

The original institution of a Purohita, as the spiritual adviser of a king or a chief, need not be regarded as the sign of a far advanced hierarchical system. The position of the Brahmans must have been a peculiar one in India from the very beginning. They appear from the very first as a class of men of higher intellectual power than EARLIEST INDICATIONS OF THE CEREMONIAL. 489 the rest of the Aryan colonists ; and their general position, if at all recognised, could hardly have been different from that of Vasishtha in the camp of Sudas. The hymns, therefore, which only allude to a Purohita, or priests in general, need not be ascribed to a late age. But when we meet in certain hymns, not only with these, but with various grades of priests, we may be sure that such hymns belong to the Mantra period, and not to the age of primitive Vedic poetry.

This is a question of degree. If we find such verses as " the singers sing thee, the chaunters chaunt thee *," where the singers are called not by their technical name of Udgatri, but Gayatrins, and the chaunters not by their technical name of Hotri, but Arkins, all we can say is that the later division of the sacrifice between Hotri and Udgatri priests is here found in its first elements. It does not follow that there existed at that time two recognised classes of priests, still less that the Udgatris were then in possession of their own Sanhita. But in Rv. v. 44. 14. we read :

" The Rich verses long for the god who watches ; the Saman verses go to him who watches ; this Soma libation calls for him that watches : I, 0 Agni, am at home in thy friendship." 2 Here it is clear that the distinction between Rich verses, that were recited, and Saman verses, that were « Rv. i. 10. TTPqfa c^T TT^^tS^W^f^W: I sft" 5RIT fW*r ^N? ^T^ fT3r^?f%T ¥^ ^fafTMl Rv. viii. 3. 22. v«*iraTOtll 490 EARLIEST INDICATIONS OF THE CEREMONIAL.
sung, must have been established, though again we need not go so far as to maintain the actual existence of a prayer-book for the Udgatri priests.

The third class of priests, the Adhvaryus, who performed the principal acts of the sacrifice, are likewise alluded to in the hymns. We read, Rv. iii. 36. 10 : " Accept, 0 Indra, what is offered thee from the hand of the Adhvaryu, or the sacrifice of the libation of the Hotri."

There are several hymns which contain allusions to the Darsapurnamasa, the famous New and Full Moon sacrifices. These sacrifices in themselves may have been of the greatest antiquity, as old as any attempt at a regulated worship of the gods. Passages therefore, where we only meet with allusions to the phases of the moon, and their recurrent appeal to the human heart to render thanks to the unknown Powers that rule the changes of nature, and the chances of human life, prove by no means, as the Indian commentators suppose, that at the time of the ancient Yedic poets the lunar ceremonies were of the same solemn and complicated nature as in later times. We read, Rv. i. 194. 4: " Let us bring fuel, let us prepare oblations remembering thee at each conjunction of the moon.1 1 I translate parva by conjunction, because parvani, the dual, is used for the full and new moon ; Asvalayana-sutras, i. 3. 12. Mr. Weigle, in his interesting article on Canarese literature (Zeitschrift der Deutschen Morgenlandischen Gesellschaft), states that habba or pabba means a festival in Canarese, whereas in Sanskrit its usual signification is a chapter of a book. Mr. Weigle therefore refers pabba to a class of words, which, in being transferred from the Sanskrit into the Dravidian languages, have changed their meaning. We see, however, that the old meaning of parva new and full moon, would account very well for the meaning attached to pabba in Canarese, a festival.

INDICATIONS OF THE CEREMONIAL. 491 Do thou perfect our sacred acts that we may live long. Let us not fail in thy friendship, 0 Agni." } Passages like this do not necessitate the admission of a full-grown ceremonial, they only point to its natural beginnings. The same remark applies to the three daily prayers, at sun-rise, noon, and sun-set. Nature herself suggests these three periods as the most appropriate for rendering thanks to the heavenly givers of light and life. Thus Manu Vaivasvata2 alludes several times to the three periods of the day which the gods themselves have fixed for their sacrifice, sun-rise (surya udyati, or sura udite), mid-day (madhyandine divah, or madhyandine), and sun-set (nimruchi, or atuchi), and he calls this established order of the sacrifice rita, the law or the truth.

But when these sacrifices are mentioned with their technical names, when the morning, and noon, and evening prayers are spoken of as first, second, and third libation, we feel that we move in a different at- 1 wjiNr wu^ro ^ftf% ^ fwm: *rwt tpfwT ^h 2 Rv. viii. 27. 19 : —
sra rift *rc#r f^^^ ^q -m^r* *rsi ^tii^°ii <=rm ^rer *re^ f^^spft ^¥ttt^ ^fmii^ii 492 INDICATIONS OF THE CEREMONIAL.
mosphere, and that we are listening to priests rather than to poets. Thus Rv. iii. 28. 1 :
" Agni, accept our offering, the cake, 0 Jatavedas, at the morning libation, thou rich in prayer.
"The baked cake, 0 Agni, is prepared for thee alone indeed ; accept it, 0 youngest of all the gods.
u Agni, eat the cake, offered to thee when the day is over, thou art the son of strength, stationed at the sacrifice.
" At the mid-day libation, 0 Jatavedas, accept here the cake, 0 sage ! Agni, the wise do not diminish at the sacrifices the share of thee, who art great.
11 Agni, as thou lovest at the third libation the cake, 0 son of strength, that is offered to thee, therefore, moved by our praise, take this precious oblation to the immortal gods to rouse them.
" Agni, thou who art growing, accept, 0 Jatavedas, the offering, the cake, at the close of day"

This hymn contains in reality nothing but a set of invocations for the three daily libations ; it uses the very words used in the ceremonial, and it would hardly have been written except by some pious priest brought up under the system of the Brahmanic ceremonial.

The technical names of the priests are of frequent occurrence. The name of Ritvij would not prove a great development of the ceremonial. It would only mean the priest who officiates at the various seasons. It was then that the sacred fire was kindled by friction. It was lighted in the morning day after day (dive dive), it was lighted at the full and- new moon, and it was lighted likewise at each of the great natural divisions of the year. Thus it is said, Rv.
DANASTUTIS. 493 iii. 29. 10 : " This wood is thy mother every season, born from which thou shonest. Do thou sit there, as thou knowest it, and make our prayers prosper."

There is nothing artificial in this. But when we meet with the names of the Ritvij priests, such as Potri, Agnidhra, Prasastri, Neshtri, Hotri, Adhvaryu, Brahman *, we can no longer doubt that here we have to deal with late and artificial poetry. These names of priests are afterwards still further generalised, and transferred to Agni, who, as the god of fire, is supposed to carry the offerings of men to the seats of the gods. He is called the Purohita, or high-priest. Sapta-hotri also, and sapta-manusha, acting as seven priests2, are names applied to the god of the sacrificial flame.

There is a whole class of hymns commonly called ddnastutis, or praises of gifts. They are the thanksgivings of certain priests for presents received from their royal patrons. All of these, like the Latin panegyrics, betray a modern character, and must be referred to the Mantra period. In the Brahmana period, however, not only are these panegyrics known, but the liberality of these royal patrons is held up to the admiration and imitation of later generations by stories which had to be repeated at the sacrifices. In the Saiikhayana-sutras (xvi. 11.), the following stories called Narasansa (neuter), are mentioned as fit for such occasions. The story of Sunahsepha ; of Ivakshivat Ausija who received gifts from Svanaya Bha-vyaya ; of Syavasva Archananasa who received gifts from Vaidadasvi ; of Bharadvaja who received gifts 2 These seven priests seem to be Hotri, Potri, Neshtri, Agnidh, Prasastri, Adhvaryu and Brahman 494 SATIRICAL HYMNS.

from Bribu the carpenter, and Prastoka Sarnjaya ; of Vasishtha who was Purohita of King Sudas Paijavana ; of Medhathi, and how Asanga Playogi having been a woman became a man ; of Vatsa Kanva who received gifts from Tirindira Parasavyaya; of Vasa Asvya who received gifts from Prithusravas Kanina; of Praskanva who received gifts from Prishadhra Medhya Matarisva (sic) ; of Nabhanedishtha Manava, who received gifts from the AngirasV All these acts of royal liberality are recorded in the hymns of the Kig-veda, but the hymns themselves may safely be referred to the second age of Vedic poetry.

Another and most convincing proof that some of our hymns belong to a secondary period of Vedic poetry, is contained in a song, ascribed to Vasishtha, in which the elaborate ceremonial of the Brahmans is actually turned into ridicule. The 103rd hymn in the 7th Mandala, which is called a panegyric of the frogs, is clearly a satire on the priests ; and it is curious to observe that the same animal should have been chosen by the Vedic satirist to represent the priests, which by the earliest satirist of Greece was selected as the representative of the Homeric heroes.

" After lying prostrate for a year, like Brahmans performing a vow, the frogs have emitted their voice, roused by the showers of heaven. When the heavenly waters fell upon them as upon a dry fish lying in a pond, the music of the frogs comes together, like the lowing of cows with their calves.

"When, at the approach of the rainy season, the rain has wetted them, as they were longing and thirsting, one goes to the other while he talks, like a son to his father, saying, akkhala (fipsxsxs!; xoa£xoa£.)
SATIRICAL HYMNS. 495 " One of them embraces the other, when they revel in the shower of water, and the brown frog jumping after he has been ducked, joins his speech with the green one.
zh: 3 「婆利訶斯跋蒂」，《愛達羅耶婆羅門書》（Aitareya-brahmana）云，「為眾神之祭祀主，人間國王之祭祀主乃其繼承者。」* i i 4 488 祭祀主。
「在一位祭司行走於其前的國王，能在自己的家中建立穩固的地位；大地永遠為他效力，人民也會主動向他低眉順眼。」
「無人可擋，他奪取寶藏，取自敵人與友人的寶藏，他本身是國王，向婆羅門賜予禮物；諸神保護他。」

這表明婆羅門在剎帝利（Kshatriya）國王宮廷中的地位比起單純的祭司要更具影響力。他們走在國王之前，並認為自己優越於國王。到了後來，當儀式的執行不再由淨行者（Purohita）擔綱時，首席祭司取代了所謂的婆羅門祭司的地位。婆羅門祭司是整個祭祀的主教（episcopos），儘管他本人在其中的主動參與甚少。因此在《艾塔雷亞梵書》（Aitareya-brahmana）第七卷第十六節所述的「哈利沙杌陀羅」（Harischandra）祭祀中，阿雅斯亞（Ayasya）擔任頌唱祭司（Udgatri），贍摩達格尼（Jamadagni）擔任行祭祭司（Adhvaryu），毗濕婆密多羅（Visvamitra）擔任火祭祭司（Hotri），而瓦西什塔（Vasishtha）──以伊克什瓦庫（Ikshvaku）王朝的淨行者聞名──擔任婆羅門祭司。在《泰提利亞梵經》（Taittiriya-sanhita）第三卷第五節第二段中，我們讀到：「人類誕生時擁有一位瓦西什塔為淨行者，因此應當選擇一位瓦西什塔為婆羅門祭司。」在《艾塔雷亞梵書》中，婆羅門祭司再次被認同於婆羅笈多（Brihaspati），他是諸神的淨行者（pura-etri）。

普羅希塔（Purohita）的原初制度，即作為國王或首領的精神顧問，毋庸視之為等級制度已高度發達的標誌。婆羅門在印度的地位從最初就必然具有其獨特性。他們從一開始就表現為智識力量高於其他雅利安殖民者的一類人；若其地位得到承認，其位置應當與蘇陀斯（Sudas）軍營中的瓦西什塔（Vasishtha）的位置相去無幾。因此，那些僅提及普羅希塔或一般祭司的讚歌，無須歸屬於較晚的時代。但當我們在某些讚歌中遇見不僅有這些人物，而且有各種等級的祭司時，我們便可確信這類讚歌屬於咒語時期（Mantra period），而非原始吠陀詩歌的時代。

這是一個程度的問題。如果我們發現這樣的詩句：「歌者歌頌汝，誦者誦讚汝 *」，其中歌者不是以其專門術語烏德噶特里（Udgatri）稱呼，而是稱為蓋亞特林人（Gayatrins），誦者也不是以其專門術語荷特里（Hotri）稱呼，而是稱為阿金人（Arkins），我們只能說，後來在荷特里與烏德噶特里祭司之間進行的祭祀分工，在此已見其初步形態。由此並不能推論當時存在著兩個公認的祭司階級，更遑論烏德噶特里祭司當時已擁有自己的三昧多（Sanhita）。但在《梨俱吠陀》第五卷第四十四讚第十四節，我們讀到：
「梨俱吠陀詩句渴望那位守護者之神；娑摩吠陀詩句趨向那位守護者；這壇蘇摩祭祀呼喚那位守護者：我啊，阿格尼，安居於你的友誼之中。」¹ 在此可以明確看出，被誦唱的梨俱吠陀詩句與被〔演唱的〕娑摩吠陀詩句之間的區分² ¹ 《梨俱吠陀》第一卷第十首。 ² 《梨俱吠陀》第八卷第三首第二十二頌。
所唱誦的內容必然已被確立，儘管我們無需主張為烏德噶特里（Udgatri）祭司確實存在過禱告書。
第三類祭司——阿德瓦尤（Adhvaryus），他們執行祭祀的主要儀式，同樣在讚歌中被提及。我們在《梨俱吠陀》第三卷第三十六首第十節讀到：「因陀羅啊，請接納阿德瓦尤之手所獻之物，或霍特里之奠祭的祭祀。」

有好幾首讚歌包含對達爾薩普爾納瑪薩（Darsapurnamasa）的典故，即著名的新月與滿月祭祀。這些祭祀本身可能具有最悠久的古老性，與任何有組織的神祇崇拜嘗試一樣古老。因此，僅涉及月相典故的段落，以及月相對人心的週期性呼籲——促使人感謝主宰自然變化與人生禍福的未知力量——根本不能證明（如印度註釋家所主張的）在古代吠陀詩人的時代，月亮儀式具有與後世同樣莊嚴且複雜的性質。我們在《梨俱吠陀》第一卷第 194 首第 4 節讀到：「讓我們帶來木柴，讓我們準備祭品，在每次月合時記住你。」¹ ¹ 我以「月合」譯 parva，因為雙數形式 parvani 用於滿月和新月；見《阿濕婆拉延那經》第一卷第 3 節第 12 節。韋格爾先生在其關於卡納雷塞文學的有趣文章（刊於《德國東方學會雜誌》）中指出，habba 或 pabba 在卡納雷塞語中意為節慶，而在梵文中其通常含義是書的一章。韋格爾先生因此將 pabba 歸入一類詞彙，這類詞彙從梵文轉移到德拉威語族的語言時改變了含義。然而，我們發現 parva 的原始含義「新月與滿月」足以充分解釋 pabba 在卡納雷塞語中所附帶的含義——節慶。

「求汝完成我們的聖禮，俾我們得以長壽。願我們莫失汝之友誼，哦阿格尼！」} 像這樣的段落不必然要求我們承認一個完全成熟的儀式體系，它們只不過指向儀式的自然起源。同樣的評論也適用於一天三次的禱告——在日出、正午和日落時。大自然本身就啟示了這三個時段乃是向天上光明與生命的賜予者獻謝的最為恰當的時刻。因此摩奴‧瓦伊瓦斯瓦塔（Manu Vaivasvata）多次提及由眾神自身確定的一日三時段，用於他們的祭祀——日出時分（surya udyati 或 sura udite）、正午時分（madhyandine divah 或 madhyandine）和日落時分（nimruchi 或 atuchi）——他將這套既定的祭祀秩序稱為利他（rita），意為法則或真理。
但當這些祭祀以其技術性名稱被提及，當晨禱、午禱和晚禱被稱作第一、第二和第三奠祭時，我們感到自己進入了一個截然不同的領域—
抱歉，我注意到您提供的「英文原文」包含了大量的亂碼字符（*rc#r f^^^* 等），這些並非英文文本，而是顯示故障或編碼錯誤的結果。 請您重新提供清晰的英文原文，我將能夠按照您的規則準確地翻譯成繁體中文。 若您有原文掃描檔或數位版本，建議： - 重新複製英文文本 - 確認編碼格式（UTF-8） - 清除任何損壞的字符 感謝您的理解。
圍繞著我們的氛圍，而我們聽的是祭司的言辭而非詩人的聲音。例如《梨俱吠陀》第三卷第二十八讚第一節：
「阿格尼，請接納我們的供養——那個餅糕，啊多識之人，在晨間奠酒之時，你富有禱詞之力。」
「烤好的蛋糕啊，阿格尼（Agni），確實是為你一人而備；請接受它吧，啊眾神中最年輕的者。
啊，阿格尼，當白晝已盡之時，食此獻予汝之餅，汝乃力之子，駐守於祭祀。
「在正午祭祀時，啊，火神焦塔韋達斯（Jatavedas），請在此接受供餅，啊，賢者！火神，智者在祭祀中不會減損你的份額，你乃偉大之者。
阿格尼啊，正如你在第三次奠酒時鍾愛獻予你的餅糕一樣，啊！力之子啊，因此，為我們的讚頌所感動，請將這珍貴的祭品獻給不朽的諸神，以喚醒他們。
「阿格尼（Agni），汝在增長，願汝‧耶他韋達斯（Jatavedas），於日暮時接納此祭品與祭餅。」
這首讚歌實際上只不過是為三次日常奠酒禮而作的一連串祈禱文；它使用的正是婆羅門祭祀儀式中所用的詞彙，除了在婆羅門祭祀體系下養成的虔誠祭司，幾乎不可能有人會創作這樣的作品。
祭司的技術性名稱頻繁出現。里特維吉（Ritvij）這個名稱並不足以證明儀式已有重大發展。它只是指在各個季節主持祭祀的祭司。當時聖火是通過摩擦生火而點燃的。它在每天的早晨被點燃（dive dive），在滿月和新月時被點燃，同樣也在一年中每個重大自然分界處被點燃。因此經文云，梨俱吠陀（Rv.）
吠陀讚頌詩。《梨俱吠陀》第三卷第29首第10節：「此木乃汝母親，四時皆然，汝由之而生，汝乃照耀。願汝坐於其中，如汝所知者，並使我等祈禱得以興盛。」

這裡面沒有什麼人為的成分。但當我們遇到梨俱吠陀祭祀僧侶（Ritvij priests）的名稱，例如波多里（Potri）、阿格尼陀羅（Agnidhra）、普拉薩斯特里（Prasastri）、涅什特里（Neshtri）、霍特里（Hotri）、阿特瓦尤（Adhvaryu）、婆羅門（Brahman）*時，我們就再也不能懷疑，此處我們所應對的乃是後出的、人為的詩歌。這些祭祀僧侶的名稱後來進一步被概括化，並移用於阿格尼（Agni）身上，阿格尼作為火神，被認為是將人類的祭祀供品傳遞到眾神的座位。他被稱為普羅西塔（Purohita），即大祭司。薩普塔─霍特里（Sapta-hotri）與薩普塔─瑪努沙（sapta-manusha）作為七位祭司而發揮作用，也是應用於祭祀火焰之神的名稱²。

有整個一類的讚歌通常被稱為 dānastutiș（禮物讚頌），或稱為獻禮的讚美。這些是某些祭司為感謝從王族贊助人那裡獲得的禮物而獻上的感恩詞。所有這些讚歌，如同拉丁文的頌詞（panegyrics）一樣，都顯現出現代的特徵，必須歸屬於曼陀羅時期。然而，在梵書時期，不僅這些頌詞為人所知，而且這些王族贊助人的慷慨大方也被那些必須在祭祀時重複誦讀的故事所呈現，用以供後代欽仰與效法。在《商卡亞那經書》（Śāñkhāyana-sutras）（xvi. 11.）中，以下稱為那羅散沙（Narasaṃsa，中性）的故事被提及為適合於此等場合。蘇那赫舍法的故事；伊瓦克希瓦特‧奧西亞從斯瓦納亞‧巴維亞那裡獲得禮物的故事；西亞瓦絲瓦‧阿闍那那沙從瓦依達達絲維那裡獲得禮物的故事；婆羅多闍獲得禮物的故事。這七位祭司似乎是 Hotri、Potri、Neshtri、Agnidh、Prasastri、Adhvaryu 和 Brahman。

來自木匠布里布（Bribu）與普拉斯托卡‧薩姆亞亞（Prastoka Sarnjaya）；來自瓦西什塔（Vasishtha），他是蘇達斯‧派亞瓦納王（King Sudas Paijavana）的家祭司（Purohita）；來自梅達蒂（Medhathi），以及阿桑加‧普拉約吉（Asanga Playogi）如何從女身轉變為男身；來自瓦茨薩‧康瓦（Vatsa Kanva），他從蒂林迪拉‧帕拉薩維亞亞（Tirindira Parasavyaya）獲得贈禮；來自瓦薩‧阿斯維亞（Vasa Asvya），他從普里圖斯拉瓦斯‧卡尼納（Prithusravas Kanina）獲得贈禮；來自普拉斯康瓦（Praskanva），他從普里沙德拉‧梅德亞‧瑪塔里斯瓦（Prishadhra Medhya Matarisva）獲得贈禮；來自那巴涅迪什塔‧瑪納瓦（Nabhanedishtha Manava），他從昂吉拉斯族（Angiras）獲得贈禮。所有這些王家慷慨的行為皆錄於*梨俱吠陀*的讚歌中，然而這些讚歌本身可以安全地推溯至吠陀詩歌的第二個時代。
另一個極具說服力的證據，足以證明我們某些讚歌屬於吠陀詩歌的次級時期，乃是出自一首歸屬於婆私吒（Vasishtha）的詩歌，其中婆羅門繁複的儀式禮儀實際上遭到嘲笑。第七曼陀羅中的第一〇三首讚歌被稱為「青蛙的頌辭」，明顯是對祭司的諷刺；有趣的是，同一種動物竟然被吠陀諷刺詩人選來代表祭司，而這同一種動物也曾被古希臘最早的諷刺詩人選來代表荷馬英雄們。
「蛙類如同修行願誓的婆羅門一般，俯伏一年之久，至此經天降甘霖喚醒，而發出聲音。當天上之水如雨落於彼等，如同落於池中乾涸之魚身上時，蛙鳴之聲匯聚一處，猶如乳牛與其犢牛的哀鳴相和。」
「當雨季臨近，雨水浸潤了它們——正如它們渴望與企盼的那樣——一個向另一個靠近，彼此交談，如同兒子向父親說話般，說著『akkhala』(fipsxsxs!; xoa£xoa£.)」
諷刺詩篇。495「其中一隻擁抱另一隻，當牠們在水雨中盡興作樂時，那隻棕色的青蛙在被浸沒之後跳躍著，用牠的聲音與綠色的青蛙相和。
